factual-accuracy.requirement: States individualism scores: South Korea collectivist (IDV 18±5), Finland individualist (IDV 63±5), Mexico collectivist (IDV 30±5)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response describes South Korea and Mexico as collectivist and Finland as more individualistic, but it does not state the required Hofstede IDV scores (Korea 18±5, Finland 63±5, Mexico 30±5). No numeric individualism values for all three countries are provided.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States power distance scores: South Korea mid-high (PDI 60±10), Finland low (PDI 33±5), Mexico very high (PDI 81±10)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response gives South Korea a power distance value (“Hofstede PDI around 60+”) but does not state Finland’s PDI around 33 (±5) or Mexico’s PDI around 81 (±10). It only describes Finland and Mexico qualitatively as low/high power distance without the required numeric score ranges.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States uncertainty avoidance: South Korea very high (UAI 85±10), Finland moderate (UAI 59±10), Mexico high (UAI 82±10)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses power distance and collectivism for South Korea, Finland, and Mexico but never states uncertainty avoidance values or ranges for any of the three countries. It does not mention UAI at all, nor provide the specified figures (Korea ~85, Finland ~59, Mexico ~82).

factual-accuracy.requirement: Describes Classting as teacher-moderated or teacher-led class SNS with private counseling rooms/secret channels and coarse moderation controls
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response describes Classting as a social media–style classroom platform with strong teacher moderation and includes “Private Counseling” as a private/anonymous channel for students to contact teachers, aligning with teacher-led SNS and private counseling-room concepts.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Wilma has 95-98% coverage in Finnish basic education schools and handles attendance/grades/messaging
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response describes Wilma as Finland’s dominant platform and notes it supports scheduling, attendance, and messaging, but it does not state that Wilma has 95–98% coverage in Finnish basic education schools. The specific coverage percentage range is missing, so the full criterion is not satisfied.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Mentions Google Classroom Mexico/Latin America deployments reached state scale (hundreds of thousands to 1M+ students) with private comments and teacher-controlled posting emphasized
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses Google Classroom adoption in Mexico/Latin America and mentions teacher moderation and private feedback generally, but it does not state deployments reached state scale (hundreds of thousands to 1M+ students) nor does it specifically emphasize Google Classroom features like private comments and teacher-controlled posting.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States anonymous peer assessment increases cognitive/higher-order feedback or improves final grades significantly versus identified review
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses that anonymous feedback can improve honesty, reduce anxiety, and enhance participation, but it does not state that anonymous peer assessment increases cognitive/higher-order feedback or significantly improves final grades compared with identified review.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States anonymous question queues or LAQQ reduce apprehension and increase question submission versus public-only channels
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that moderated, anonymous feedback mechanisms in high power-distance collectivist cultures “enhance student participation and reduce anxiety,” and recommends “anonymous private feedback channels” to encourage candid communication, implying reduced apprehension and increased question/help submission versus public channels.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Explains that high power distance reduces willingness to publicly challenge peers or request help in digital learning environments
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly links high power distance (in South Korea and Mexico) to students being reluctant to publicly challenge peers/teachers and to request help openly, noting that anonymous/private channels and teacher mediation can reduce this barrier in digital learning environments.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Recommends masked/anonymous early drafts, teacher-approved public showcases, anonymous live Q&A, or teacher-moderated boards for Korean group workspaces
factual-accuracy.explanation: For South Korea, the response recommends anonymous/private feedback channels and strong teacher moderation (e.g., “anonymous private feedback channels” and “teacher moderation tools”), which aligns with masked/anonymous drafts and teacher-moderated boards. It does not explicitly mention teacher-approved public showcases or anonymous live Q&A, but it does recommend anonymous and teacher-moderated workspace patterns for Korean group workspaces.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Recommends named edits/contributions, visible cursors, open collaboration, or student self-organization for Finnish group workspaces
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response’s Finland section recommends enabling “teacher-student dialogue and peer interactions” and adding “tools for reflection and peer review” in group project workspaces, which implies open collaboration and student self-organization features for Finnish contexts.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Recommends private-first design, mobile/offline editing, teacher-controlled visibility, or teacher-only posting initially for Mexican group workspaces
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response recommends Mexico-specific design choices such as teacher-led moderation, private correction/feedback channels, and structured teacher oversight of collaboration, which align with teacher-controlled visibility/teacher-only posting initially and a private-first approach for group workspaces.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Recommends double-blind or anonymous peer review with rubric-guided stems or structured feedback prompts for Korea
factual-accuracy.explanation: For South Korea, the response recommends anonymous feedback channels and teacher-moderated feedback spaces, and it explicitly suggests using anonymous private feedback to support face-saving norms. However, it does not recommend double-blind peer review nor mention rubric-guided stems or structured feedback prompts as part of the Korea-specific peer review design.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Recommends named peer review with feedback literacy scaffolding, exemplars, or process-focused critique for Finland
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response’s Finland section suggests embedding formative, process-level feedback tools and mentions adding tools for reflection and peer review, but it does not recommend named peer review nor provide feedback-literacy scaffolding, exemplars, or a process-focused critique workflow specifically for Finland.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Recommends two-tier identity (anonymous to peers, identified to teacher) or private-first peer review with structured stems for Mexico
factual-accuracy.explanation: For Mexico, the response recommends avoiding anonymous peer feedback and instead emphasizes teacher-led moderation and private correction channels. It does not recommend a two-tier identity model (anonymous to peers but identified to teacher) nor a private-first peer review flow with structured feedback stems.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Claims Finland has higher power distance than Korea or Mexico, or claims Mexico has lower power distance than Finland
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response describes Finland as low power distance and both South Korea and Mexico as high power distance, and it does not claim Finland has higher power distance than Korea or Mexico nor that Mexico has lower power distance than Finland.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Predicts anonymity or power-distance interventions will show stronger effects in high-PDI markets (Korea/Mexico) versus low-PDI (Finland)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response repeatedly links high power distance contexts (South Korea and Mexico) with greater usefulness of anonymous/private feedback and strong teacher moderation, while describing Finland’s low power distance as not needing anonymity and supporting more open feedback. This implies anonymity/power-distance interventions would have stronger effects in Korea/Mexico than Finland.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Describes temporal rollout strategies (private-first then gradual opening) with triggers or norms that enable stage transitions
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response discusses cultural differences and interface patterns (anonymity, moderation, public vs private correction) but does not describe a temporal rollout plan (e.g., private-first then gradual opening) or specify triggers/norms for moving between stages. There are no stage-transition criteria such as engagement thresholds, teacher readiness, or incident rates that would enable opening features over time.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Identifies negative consequences of recommended patterns (e.g., fairness concerns) and proposes compensating design elements or safeguards
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response notes potential downsides like public embarrassment/face-loss and reduced participation in high power-distance contexts, and it proposes safeguards such as anonymous/private feedback channels, teacher moderation tools, and avoiding public correction displays to mitigate these risks.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Specifies measurable outcomes beyond raw counts (higher-order comments, revision adoption rates) with expected effect ranges from research
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response discusses engagement and interaction mostly qualitatively and repeatedly notes a lack of direct platform-specific quantitative evidence. It does not specify measurable outcomes beyond raw counts (e.g., higher-order comment quality, revision adoption rates) nor provide expected effect ranges drawn from research. No numeric ranges or effect sizes are given for these higher-order outcomes.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Connects technical constraints (offline capability, bandwidth limits) to required collaboration design patterns in specific markets
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response discusses cultural interaction patterns (anonymity, moderation, public vs private correction) but does not connect technical constraints like offline capability or bandwidth limits to specific collaboration design patterns in South Korea, Finland, or Mexico. There are no market-specific recommendations tied to connectivity realities (e.g., low-bandwidth modes, async-first workflows). Thus the required technical-to-design linkage is missing.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Draws explicit connections between existing platform capabilities (private channels, moderation toggles) and cultural requirements they address
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly links platform features to cultural needs, e.g., Classting’s “Private Counseling” (a private/anonymous channel) to face-saving norms and high power distance, and repeatedly ties high teacher moderation/oversight in Classting and Google Classroom use to hierarchy/harmony requirements in Korea and Mexico.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Notes contexts where cultural predictions don't hold or explicitly flags inferences versus direct empirical evidence
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response repeatedly qualifies claims by noting missing direct empirical measures (e.g., “direct empirical measures… are lacking” and a “scarcity of direct empirical studies”) and separates cultural generalizations from evidence in the “Limitations and Research Gaps” section.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Provides identical design recommendations for all three markets without adapting interaction patterns to their different PDI or collectivism profiles
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response differentiates recommendations by market (e.g., anonymous/teacher-moderated feedback for Korea, transparency and low moderation for Finland, teacher-led moderation and limited anonymity for Mexico). It explicitly ties these differences to varying power distance and collectivism profiles rather than giving one uniform design pattern.

presentation-quality.requirement: Organizes analysis by feature or market with consistent comparative structure (e.g., Korea/Finland/Mexico breakdowns per feature)
presentation-quality.explanation: The response consistently compares South Korea, Finland, and Mexico across the same set of features (anonymous feedback, teacher moderation, public vs private correction, real-time collaboration, willingness to challenge, engagement), including a comparative table that organizes the analysis by market with a parallel structure.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses precise UX/product design terminology (interaction patterns, moderation controls, engagement metrics, feature toggles)
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses UX/product design terminology such as interaction patterns, anonymous feedback mechanisms, teacher moderation, public vs private correction displays, real-time collaboration features, and references engagement/peer interaction/assignment completion metrics.

presentation-quality.requirement: Maintains objective, research-oriented tone connecting design recommendations to empirical findings without marketing claims
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses an analytical, research-report style and repeatedly ties design implications (e.g., anonymity, teacher moderation, private corrections) to cited cultural constructs like power distance, collectivism, and face-saving, while noting evidence gaps. It avoids promotional language and marketing claims, maintaining an objective tone throughout.

presentation-quality.requirement: Presents engagement effects with specific metrics or ranges (e.g., percentage increases, effect sizes) rather than only qualitative descriptions
presentation-quality.explanation: The response mostly discusses engagement and interaction effects qualitatively (e.g., “increased engagement,” “improves honesty,” “reduces anxiety”) and explicitly notes a lack of direct empirical, platform-specific quantitative measures. It does not provide concrete engagement metrics or ranges (percent changes, effect sizes) tied to the interaction patterns across the contexts.

presentation-quality.requirement: Restates the same cultural insight or design recommendation multiple times using different wording without adding new analysis
presentation-quality.explanation: The response repeatedly reiterates the same recommendations—use anonymous/private feedback in Korea and Mexico due to face-saving, and rely on teacher moderation in high power-distance contexts—across the platform sections, comparison table, design implications, and conclusion with only minor rewording and little added analysis.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites Hofstede cultural dimensions framework for individualism and power distance scores across the three countries
citation-quality.explanation: The response references Hofstede and mentions power distance qualitatively (e.g., “Hofstede PDI around 60+” for South Korea), but it does not provide Hofstede individualism and power distance scores across all three countries (South Korea, Finland, and Mexico). Finland and Mexico Hofstede IDV/PDI scores are not cited or listed.

citation-quality.requirement: References official documentation or deployment reports for all 3 platforms: Classting, Wilma, and Google Classroom deployments
citation-quality.explanation: The response includes cited sources for Classting (e.g., a Newswire article and company profile), Wilma (e.g., Nordenbladet and Helsinki Times), and Google Classroom/Latin America deployments (e.g., Google for Education customer stories and support/community pages), thereby referencing documentation or deployment reports for all three platforms.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites at least 2 academic sources on anonymous peer assessment, peer review, or participation mechanisms in educational contexts
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites multiple academic sources related to anonymous peer assessment/peer feedback, including Mäkipää & Hildén (2021) and Zhang et al. (2023), and also references a University of Helsinki publication on peer feedback.

citation-quality.requirement: Includes K-12 or secondary education research on cultural differences, engagement patterns, or digital collaboration
citation-quality.explanation: The response explicitly focuses on secondary (K-12) contexts in South Korea, Finland, and Mexico and discusses cultural differences (power distance, collectivism), engagement patterns, and digital collaboration/peer feedback features across platforms like Classting, Wilma, and Google Classroom.

citation-quality.requirement: Uses Reddit, teacher forums, or social media as primary evidence for behavioral patterns or platform effectiveness
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites academic papers, news articles, blogs, and official Google support/resources, but it does not use Reddit, teacher forums, or social media as primary evidence for behavioral patterns or platform effectiveness.
